content.text: The Handoff Document: What It Must Contain and Why

An on-call handoff is a knowledge transfer under time pressure. The incoming engineer has 30 minutes to understand the current state of a production system they did not personally operate for the past week. Every gap in that knowledge becomes a blind spot in their first hours on-call — the highest-risk period, since they are unfamiliar with the current system state.

A structured handoff document removes ambiguity. Instead of 'let me know if anything comes up,' the outgoing engineer writes down exactly what is elevated, what is broken, what is scheduled, and what shortcuts are currently in place. The incoming engineer confirms receipt. The handoff is complete when the incoming engineer acknowledges — not when the outgoing engineer sends the message.

attrs.alt: Handoff document anatomy with six required sections, blocking conditions, runbook structure, and four runbook health states

content.text: Required Sections of a Handoff Document
Section
Contents
Required?
1. Budget Snapshot
Current remaining % per SLO; freeze status; burn rate trend
Always
2. Open Incidents
All P1/P2 tickets: summary, owner, current status, expected resolution
Always (can be 'none')
3. Scheduled Deploys
High-risk releases this week: description, type, contact, risk level
Always (can be 'none')
4. Known Fragility
Any subsystem in degraded state; active workarounds; monitoring to watch
If any exist
5. Runbook Updates
Links to runbooks changed or created this shift
If any exist
6. Acknowledgement
Incoming on-call confirms they have read and understood the document
Always — blocks pager transfer until received
Handoff Document Implementation

The has_blocking_conditions() method returns a list of issues that must be resolved before the pager transfers. The outgoing engineer retains the pager until the incoming engineer has acknowledged or the blocking conditions are resolved. This prevents the failure mode where a P1 incident is handed off asynchronously and the incoming on-call does not read the Slack message before their first page arrives.
Runbook Requirements

A runbook is not documentation — it is a decision tree for a specific alert, written so that an engineer who has never seen that failure before can reduce user impact in under 15 minutes. This distinction matters: documentation explains how a system works; a runbook tells the on-call engineer exactly what to do when it fails.
Required Runbook Sections
Every deployed alert rule must have a corresponding runbook. The runbook must contain these sections before the alert is deployed to production.
Alert metadata: name, SLO affected, severity, alert condition, last reviewed date, owner team
Symptoms: what users experience; what metrics look like at alert time
Immediate mitigation: copy-paste commands that reduce impact without requiring diagnosis first
Root cause checklist: ordered steps to identify the cause — stop at the first positive result
Escalation: when to escalate, who to page, exact contact information
Resolution verification: commands to confirm the system has returned to healthy state
Post-incident: Jira ticket instructions, post-mortem trigger criteria
Runbook Template

# runbook_template.md — GenAI Service Runbook Template
#
# One runbook per alert. File: runbooks/<alert-name>.md
# Required fields: all sections must be populated before alert is deployed.

## Alert: [ALERT_NAME]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| SLO affected | [availability / latency / eval_quality / safety] |
| Severity | [P1 / P2 / P3] |
| Alert condition | [e.g. burn rate > 14.4x for 1h AND 5m] |
| Last reviewed | [YYYY-MM-DD] |
| Owner team | [SRE / ML / Platform] |

## Symptoms

What the user sees:
- [e.g. API responses returning 503 / slow response / degraded output quality]

What metrics show:
- [e.g. error_rate_1h > 0.1% sustained for 30 min]

## Immediate Mitigation

> Run these first — no diagnosis needed. They reduce impact while you investigate.

```bash
# [Step 1: copy-paste command]
kubectl rollout undo deployment/genai-api -n production

# [Step 2: verify mitigation is working]
kubectl get deployment genai-api -n production -w
```

## Root Cause Checklist

Work through in order. Stop when you find the cause.

- [ ] 1. Check LLM provider status: https://status.openai.com
- [ ] 2. Check recent deploys: `git log --oneline -10 origin/main`
- [ ] 3. Check model version pinning: `kubectl get cm model-config -n production -o yaml`
- [ ] 4. Check eval dashboard for quality regression: [GRAFANA_LINK]
- [ ] 5. Check prompt config for recent changes: [CONFIG_REPO_LINK]

## Escalation

Escalate to ML secondary on-call if:
- Eval quality drop is confirmed and no deploy preceded it (model drift)
- Safety flag rate is elevated (always page ML secondary regardless)
- Root cause checklist exhausted without finding cause

ML secondary PagerDuty: [SCHEDULE_ID]
Provider TAM contact: [NAME, email, phone]

## Resolution Verification

```bash
# Confirm alert has resolved
curl -s $PROMETHEUS_URL/api/v1/query?query=slo:error_budget:burn_rate1h | jq '.data.result'

# Confirm no active incidents
# Check PagerDuty dashboard and Jira OPS board
```

## Post-Incident

- Create Jira ticket in OPS project if not already open
- Tag ticket with: `slo-breach` `[slo-name]`
- Post-mortem required if: P1 incident OR budget loss > 10% in one event
- Post-mortem template: [WIKI_LINK]

---
*Note: Update this runbook after every incident where it was used.*
*Runbooks older than 180 days without a review block new alert deployments.*
GenAI-Specific Runbook Sections

Standard runbook templates cover infrastructure failure modes well. GenAI services require additional sections covering the model and quality failure modes that have no analog in traditional software services.

## GenAI-Specific Runbook Section: Eval Quality Regression

### Reading the Eval Dashboard

Eval quality is reported as a 7-day rolling pass rate.
A single day's regression may not appear in the 7-day average for 24–48h.
Read the DAILY eval pass rate (not 7-day average) to diagnose current state.

Dashboard: [GRAFANA_EVAL_LINK]

Panels to check:
1. eval_pass_rate_daily — is today's rate below baseline?
2. eval_fail_categories — which test categories are failing?
3. recent_prompt_changes — were any prompts changed in last 48h?
4. model_version_current — is the model version what we expect?

### Pinning a Model Version

```bash
# Check current model config
kubectl get cm model-config -n production -o jsonpath='{.data.model_version}'

# Pin to a specific version (prevents provider auto-upgrades)
kubectl patch cm model-config -n production \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"data":{"model_version":"gpt-4o-2024-08-06"}}'

# Verify the rollout picked up the new config
kubectl rollout restart deployment/genai-api -n production
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n production
```

### Reverting a Prompt Change

Prompts are stored in: [CONFIG_REPO]/prompts/

```bash
# Find the last prompt change
cd /path/to/config-repo
git log --oneline -- prompts/

# Revert to previous commit
git revert HEAD --no-edit
git push origin main

# The config sync operator picks up the change within 60 seconds.
# Monitor: kubectl logs -l app=config-sync -n platform -f
```

### LLM Provider Escalation

Provider status pages:
- OpenAI: https://status.openai.com
- Anthropic: https://status.anthropic.com

TAM contact (business hours): [NAME], [email]
Provider P1 support ticket: [PORTAL_URL]

When to escalate to provider vs. handle internally:
- Availability drop with no recent deploys → provider incident (check status page first)
- Latency spike with no change in traffic → provider issue or model version change
- Eval quality drop with recent prompt change → internal (revert prompt)
- Safety flag spike with no config change → escalate to ML secondary first
Runbook Freshness Policy

A runbook that has not been reviewed in 6 months is likely to have broken commands, outdated contact information, and references to systems that have changed. Define explicit health states and enforce the policy through the CI/CD pipeline.
State
Age
Impact
Action
Current
< 90 days since review
None
Normal operations
Stale
90–180 days since review
Commands may have drifted
Flag in monthly SRE retro; schedule review
Expired
> 180 days since review
High risk of misleading on-call
Block new alert rule deployments for that service
Missing
No runbook for a deployed alert
On-call has no guidance
Block alert deployment until runbook exists

Wire runbook_lint.py into the CI pipeline for the alert rules repository. A missing or expired runbook causes the CI check to fail, preventing deployment of new alert rules until the runbook is created or reviewed.
The Alert-Runbook Contract

Enforce a strict rule: no alert rule ships to production without a tested runbook. This sounds obvious but commonly breaks down in two ways. First, engineers write alert rules during a reliability sprint and defer runbook writing — the alerts deploy, and the first on-call engineer to receive one of them has no guidance. Second, existing runbooks drift as the system evolves, but the alert remains unchanged. Both are caught by the lint check.

The 'immediate mitigation' section is the most valuable part of a runbook. An on-call engineer paged at 2:00 AM has one primary goal: reduce user impact as fast as possible. A command they can run without fully understanding the situation — a rollback, a feature flag disable, a circuit breaker activation — buys time for diagnosis. Write this section first, before any other section.
Common Runbook Failures
Commands that require environment setup not documented in the runbook: the on-call engineer cannot run the command without knowing which cluster context to use or which environment variables to set
Escalation contacts listed by role only: 'contact the ML team' is not actionable at 3 AM; include name, PagerDuty schedule ID, and direct Slack handle
Runbook that only explains root cause investigation, not immediate mitigation: diagnosis is secondary; impact reduction is primary

Diagnosis steps ordered by 'most interesting' rather than 'most likely': the on-call engineer should check the most common causes first — provider incident, recent deploy, model version change — before the exotic ones
GenAI runbooks missing eval dashboard instructions: SRE engineers do not intrinsically know how to read an eval pass rate graph or distinguish daily from 7-day average
Summary

On-call handoffs and runbooks are operational infrastructure, not administrative overhead. A handoff document with six required sections — budget snapshot, open incidents, scheduled deploys, known fragility, runbook updates, and acknowledgement — gives the incoming engineer the situational awareness they need to respond effectively from their first page. Runbooks must exist for every deployed alert, follow a standard structure with immediate mitigation first, and be reviewed on a 90-day freshness cycle enforced by CI/CD lint. For GenAI services, extend runbooks with sections covering eval dashboard reading, model version pinning, prompt revert procedures, and LLM provider escalation paths.
